---
title: "DSAN 5450: Data Ethics and Policy"
author: Jiahui Liu
---

# Hidden in Plain Sight: How Ambiguous Privacy Policies in Femtech Apps Undermine Informed Consent

## Abstract

This project examines whether privacy policies in femtech applications can meaningfully support informed consent. The analysis focuses on three menstrual tracking and women’s health apps: Flo, Clue, and Ovia. These apps collect highly sensitive reproductive health data, including menstrual cycle information, fertility data, pregnancy information, symptoms, sexual activity, health conditions, app usage data, and in some cases child-related, employer-related, or health-plan-related information. Because this data can reveal intimate details about a person’s body, sexuality, pregnancy status, and health choices, privacy disclosures in this context require a higher standard of clarity than ordinary app privacy policies.

The project uses the publicly available privacy policies of Flo, Clue, and Ovia as textual data. Through comparative textual analysis, each policy is coded for sensitive data collection, third-party sharing, advertising, analytics, data retention, user deletion rights, plain-language explanation, and vague or broad policy language. The project also applies the course concept of operationalization by turning the abstract idea of “meaningful informed consent” into observable indicators, such as whether a policy clearly explains what sensitive data is collected, why it is used, whether it is shared, whether it is used for advertising or analytics, and how long it is retained.

The findings show that all three apps provide formal privacy disclosures, but they differ in clarity and usability. Flo provides detailed explanations of data collection and advertising/analytics partners, but its policy is long and complex. Clue has the clearest structure because it separates account data, usage data, and health data, and repeatedly states that tracked health data is not shared with advertisers. Ovia has the broadest data environment because it includes fertility, pregnancy, parenting, health coaching, employer or health-plan services, research, and deidentified or aggregated data.

Based on these findings, this project recommends a stronger femtech privacy disclosure standard. The policy recommendation is to require standardized plain-language privacy summaries, mandatory sensitive reproductive health data labels, specific third-party sharing tables, separate opt-in consent for secondary uses, clearer retention and deletion rules, and auditable privacy claims. Overall, the project argues that privacy policies should not be treated as equivalent to meaningful informed consent. In the context of reproductive health data, consent should require clear, specific, and usable disclosure.
